title: Plain afternoon-tea scones
subtitle: Twelve light scones for the tea tray
excerpt: The plain British scone for an afternoon tea tray. Self-raising flour, cold butter rubbed in, brought together with cold milk in a few strokes. Twenty minutes from bowl to oven.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the household-baking chapter records the British plain scone shape. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): the regional variations across the West Country and the North establish milk as the canonical liquid for a plain scone (as distinct from a buttermilk biscuit). Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845): the rubbing-in method for short bakes is laid out as a household standard.

recipe.freezeNotes: Open-freeze unbaked scones on a tray; bag once solid; bake from frozen on a hot tray, adding 3 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly to 24 across two trays.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Rub butter into the flour the day before and chill the dry mix; bring together with cold milk on the morning.

recipeTools.notes: 5 cm round, dipped in flour between cuts.
For the egg or milk wash.

glossaryTerms.definition: Working cold butter into flour with the fingertips until the mixture looks like coarse breadcrumbs. The aim is small pea-sized pieces of butter coated in flour, not a smooth paste. Cold butter is essential; warm butter blends into the flour and gives a tight, biscuit-like crumb rather than the light shortened texture a scone wants.

content.text: A plain scone is a quick weekend bake: twenty minutes from bowl to oven, twelve scones from a single round of dough. The flour is self-raising, the butter cold, the milk cold, and the handling brief. The whole secret is in the rubbing-in stage and in stopping as soon as the dough comes together.
Serve with butter, clotted cream and jam. The order of cream and jam on the scone is a Devon-versus-Cornwall argument with no resolution; either is correct.

attrs.title: Cold ingredients, brief handling
attrs.body: The butter wants to be straight from the fridge; the milk wants to be cold. Handling the dough as little as possible keeps the texture light. Bring it together in around eight strokes, not eighty.

items.prepNote: beaten, for the wash (optional, gives a glossier top)

content.text: Heat the oven to 220°C fan. Line a baking tray with baking paper. Sift the {{self-raising-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} into a wide bowl. Add the {{unsalted-butter}} in cubes.
with cold fingertips until the mixture looks like coarse breadcrumbs with a few small pieces still visible. Stir in the {{caster-sugar}}.

Pour in the {{whole-milk}} all at once. Mix with a palette knife in figure-of-eight strokes until the dough just comes together; it should look rough and shaggy, not smooth. Tip onto a lightly floured surface and pat (do not roll) to a slab about 3 cm thick. Cut twelve rounds with a floured 5 cm cutter, pressing straight down without twisting.

Set the rounds on the tray with about 3 cm between each. Brush the tops with the beaten {{eggs}} or with a little more {{whole-milk}}. Bake for 10–12 minutes; the tops should be deep gold, the sides risen and dry, and the scones should sound hollow when tapped on the base. Cool on a rack for at least 5 minutes before splitting.

attrs.heading: If something goes wrong
attrs.intro: Three things that catch the plain scone out, and how to read them.

items.symptom: The scones rose poorly and feel heavy.
items.cause: The butter warmed up during rubbing-in, or the dough was overworked.
items.fix: Use cold butter, work fast, and stop as soon as the dough comes together. A few visible flecks of butter at the edges is the right look.

items.symptom: The tops are pale and the sides are pale.
items.cause: The oven was below 220°C, or the tray was too low.
items.fix: Check the oven with an oven thermometer; place the tray near the top of the oven.

items.cause: The cutter was twisted on the way down.
items.fix: Press the cutter straight down. Twisting seals the layered edges and stops the rise on that side.
